summary: American serialist composer and theorist (1916–2011). Best known for the essay 'Who Cares if You Listen?' (1958, originally titled 'The Composer as Specialist'), which defended modern music from public indifference by comparing it to advanced mathematics. Key works include Philomel (electronic piece for synthesizer and soprano). Formulated the theory of all-combinatorial hexachords. Taught at Juilliard. Frequently contrasted with John Cage as an opposite extreme of the American avant-garde.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: American serialist composer and music theorist, taught at Juilliard. Formulated the system of all-combinatorial hexachords in twelve-tone theory. Alex Ross in The Rest Is Noise wrote that Babbitt 'produced music so byzantine in construction that one practically needed a security clearance to understand it.' Frequently contrasted with John Cage as opposite extremes of the American avant-garde. Wrote the never-staged Broadway musical Fabulous Voyage. In 1948 developed a system of durations for his Composition for Four Instruments. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Who Cares if You Listen?' (1958, originally titled 'The Composer as Specialist') — argued that dismissing modern music was as absurd as dismissing advanced mathematics; published in High Fidelity
examples: This American composer argued that dismissing modern music was just as absurd as dismissing advanced mathematics in the 1958 article 'Who Cares if You Listen?'
A 1958 magazine article titled 'Who Cares if You Listen?' attracted controversy for claiming that serious music had little appeal to the public.

clues.clue: Taught at Juilliard (where the Juilliard String Quartet, which won 5 Grammys for chamber music, is based; students dual-enroll at Columbia)
examples: Milton Babbitt taught at this school [Juilliard], whose namesake string quartet has won five Grammy awards for chamber music. Many students at this school dual-enroll at Columbia University.

clues.clue: Alex Ross compared Babbitt in The Rest Is Noise: 'produced music so byzantine in construction that one practically needed a security clearance to understand it'
examples: This classical music critic for the New Yorker declared Babbitt 'produced music so byzantine in construction that one practically needed a security clearance to understand it.'

clues.clue: Contrasted with John Cage as 'two extremes of avant-garde music' (in a 1987 New York Times article)
examples: A 1987 New York Times article compared Cage with this composer of the electronic pieces Philomel and Correspondences as 'two extremes of avant-garde music.'

clues.clue: Formulated the six all-combinatorial hexachord sets in serialist theory
examples: The magic hexachord is one of the six all-combinatorial hexachord sets formulated as part of this composer's serialist theory.
This theorist's work on twelve-tone music is used in musical set theory.

clues.clue: Wrote the never-staged Broadway musical Fabulous Voyage
examples: Identify this composer behind the never-staged Broadway musical Fabulous Voyage. In 1948 he developed a system of durations for his Composition for Four Instruments.

works.name: Philomel
works.description: Electronic piece combining a synthesizer with both a live soprano and a recorded soprano, inspired by the Ovidian myth of Philomela. The soprano begins by vocalizing the syllable 'eeh' at various pitches against a synthesizer backdrop. Also described as combining electronic music with recorded soprano, inspired by the Ovid myth. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Combines synthesizer with live and recorded soprano; inspired by Ovid (the Philomela/nightingale myth); soprano begins by vocalizing 'eeh'
examples: This American serial composer combined the synthesizer with both live and recorded soprano in his Philomel.
Philomel begins with the soprano soloist simply vocalizing the syllable 'eeh' at various pitches, against the backdrop of a synthesizer and a recorded soprano.

comprehensive_summary:
Milton Babbitt (1916–2011) was an American serialist composer and music theorist. He is best known for his 1958 essay 'Who Cares if You Listen?' (originally titled 'The Composer as Specialist' — the provocative title was given by the editors of High Fidelity magazine), which defended modern music from public indifference by arguing that dismissing advanced music was as absurd as dismissing advanced mathematics. The article attracted significant controversy.

In twelve-tone theory, Babbitt formulated the six all-combinatorial hexachord sets — complementary series whose aggregates form twelve-tone rows. He also drew comparisons between music and advanced mathematics, and his work on twelve-tone music underpins musical set theory. In 1948 he developed a system of durations for his Composition for Four Instruments. He was frequently contrasted with John Cage — a 1987 New York Times article called them 'two extremes of avant-garde music.' Alex Ross, in The Rest Is Noise, wrote that Babbitt 'produced music so byzantine in construction that one practically needed a security clearance to understand it.'

Babbitt taught at the Juilliard School (whose Juilliard String Quartet won five Grammys for chamber music). He also wrote the never-staged Broadway musical Fabulous Voyage.

His best-known composition is Philomel, an electronic piece combining a synthesizer with both a live soprano and a recorded soprano. Inspired by Ovid's myth of Philomela, the soprano begins by vocalizing the syllable 'eeh' at various pitches over a synthesizer backdrop. Babbitt also wrote the electronic piece Correspondences.

cards.front: Composer: wrote 'Who Cares if You Listen?' (originally titled 'The Composer as Specialist'), published in High Fidelity (1958); argued that dismissing modern music is as absurd as dismissing advanced mathematics
Composer: formulated the six all-combinatorial hexachord sets in twelve-tone theory; his work underpins musical set theory
Composer: frequently contrasted with John Cage as 'two extremes of avant-garde music'; Alex Ross wrote that he produced music so 'byzantine' one needed a 'security clearance to understand it'
Composer: taught at Juilliard; wrote the never-staged Broadway musical Fabulous Voyage; developed a system of durations in Composition for Four Instruments (1948)
Work: electronic piece combining synthesizer with both a live and a recorded soprano; inspired by Ovid's Philomela myth; soprano begins by vocalizing 'eeh' at various pitches

recursive_suggestions: John Cage (frequent contrast figure — 4'33", Imaginary Landscapes, tape music)
Serialism / Twelve-tone technique (concept — Schoenberg, Webern, Berg, Babbitt)
